name: Lonnie Smith
abstract: Lonnie Smith (July 3, 1942 – September 28, 2021), styled as Dr. Lonnie Smith, was an American jazz Hammond B3 organist who was a member of the George Benson quartet in the 1960s.

about_quotes.speaker: George Benson
about_quotes.evidence: I called this young fellow, Lonnie Smith... went up and got Lonnie Smith... We got – he sure found us some work... Then we finally moved... to Manhattan... Every night, James Brown would come in... That was like candy for me and Lonnie.
about_quotes.doc_title: George Benson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Duffy Jackson
about_quotes.evidence: And you know I just recently recorded a CD on Milestone with Turk Mauro... And we had Dr. Lonnie Smith on organ and Jeff Grubbs... played on a few tunes.
about_quotes.doc_title: Duffy Jackson interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, October 17, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Anat Cohen
about_quotes.evidence: It’s a Dr. Lonnie Smith song, a very simple minor eight bars, the song is just powerful and simple like many songs.
about_quotes.doc_title: Anat Cohen interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, September 9, 2021

about_quotes.speaker: Phil Flanigan
about_quotes.evidence: And I’ve become more aware of other upstaters like Mel Lewis and Lonnie Smith the organist — they’re Buffalo guys.
about_quotes.doc_title: Phil Flanigan interviewed by Monk Rowe, San Diego, California, February 15, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Alicia Olatuja
about_quotes.evidence: I would see how on a resume that might look a little interesting, yeah.
about_quotes.doc_title: Alicia Olatuja interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, February 9, 2018

about_quotes.speaker: Lou Donaldson
about_quotes.evidence: Lonnie was in his band so I got both of them at the same time.
about_quotes.doc_title: Lou Donaldson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
